verdicts.reasoning:
Both answers share the same core thesis (pretraining-as-primary-lever has plateaued; scaling rerouted to RL + test-time compute) and both handle the six criteria competently: Chinchilla characterization, data wall, test-time scaling as distinct axis, clear position + steelman, and uncertainty. The decision turns on criterion 4 (cites specific evidence accurately without fabrication) and the depth/correctness of the counterargument (criterion 5).

Evidence accuracy: Answer B grounds nearly every load-bearing claim in a specific, checkable source and its specific figures are accurate to public record: Chinchilla ~20 tokens/param; Epoch's ~300T-token effective stock with the 90% CI of 100T-1000T and 2026-2032 (median ~2028) projection, plus the overtraining-pulls-it-earlier detail; o3 ARC-AGI 87.5% vs GPT-4o ~5% and FrontierMath 25.2%; o1's log-linear train/test-time relationship; Sutskever's NeurIPS 2024 "pre-training as we know it will unquestionably end"/"peak data"/"fossil fuel" quotes; GPT-4.5/Orion as "the last model built with the same pre-training method"; Epoch's ~2e29 FLOP-by-2030 and power-as-binding-constraint figures; and the CoT non-monotonicity papers. These are real and correctly characterized. B also explicitly flags which claims rest on secondary reporting vs. primary sources — an accurate, honest calibration.

Answer A is also largely correct and is admirably disciplined: it explicitly DOWN-WEIGHTS shaky figures (undisclosed GPT-5/GPT-4.5 FLOP numbers, exact benchmark-index values) and refuses to assert false precision. This is genuinely good epistemic practice and is the one dimension where A is arguably stronger. However, A's framing is built around critiquing 'three candidates' (A/B/C) that don't exist in the task — it is structured as a meta-judgment of other answers rather than a direct synthesis, which leaves several of its own factual anchors deliberately vague ('directionally attested,' 'approximate') where B supplies the actual verifiable numbers. A asserts very few hard, checkable facts of its own; it mostly adjudicates others' facts, so it has less independently-verifiable specific evidence on the table.

Counterargument quality (criterion 5): both steelman well. A's 'one-time-harvest / opex-economics' counter is sharp and arguably the single best paragraph in either answer. B's counter is also strong and, critically, it is anchored in concrete falsifiable evidence (Epoch's untried 2-OOM headroom, power as the binding constraint, Altman/Amodei statements, the flywheel of verifiable-reward self-generated data, benchmark quadrupling). B's counterargument is better evidenced even if A's is slightly more conceptually incisive.

On net, B is more correct on criterion 4 (more specific, accurate, non-fabricated, source-anchored evidence, with honest source-quality caveats) while matching A on criteria 1-3, 5, 6. A's main edge is epistemic discipline about uncertain figures, but it achieves this partly by asserting fewer hard facts and by adopting an off-task meta-review structure. B is the stronger genuine synthesis. This is not primarily a length judgment — B's win rests on accurate specific evidence, not on being longer (the two are comparable in length).

The task asks for a synthesis of evidence on whether LLM scaling laws have plateaued, covering the three axes, with a defensible position and the strongest counterargument. Both answers nail the core analytical frame (pretraining-as-primary-lever has plateaued; system-level scaling re-routed to RL + test-time compute; data wall is the binding constraint) and both decompose the question well. The decisive difference is criterion 4 (cite specific evidence accurately without fabrication) and the overall execution against the literal task.

Answer A directly executes the task as a self-contained synthesis. Its evidence is specific and largely accurate: Chinchilla ~20:1, o1's log-linear test-time scaling on AIME, GPT-4.5/Orion as the empirical inflection ("not a frontier model"), Sutskever's "peak data"/"fossil fuel of AI" at NeurIPS 2024, Epoch's ~300T-token stock and 2026-2032 exhaustion window, o3's ARC-AGI 87.5% and FrontierMath jumps, DeepSeek-R1's open reproduction, the non-monotonicity-of-CoT papers (overthinking/underthinking), and the cost-relocation-to-inference point. It also pairs this with a genuinely strong counterargument (the multiply-not-handoff point — RL can't elicit latent capability the base lacks; ~2 OOM of pretraining untried; Epoch's 2e29 FLOP-by-2030 power-bound analysis; Altman/Amodei "no wall"), and explicitly weights the bet and flags evidence-quality caveats. This satisfies all six criteria with real, attributable evidence. A few claims rest on secondary reporting, but A flags exactly that.

Answer B has a critical execution flaw: it was written as if it were JUDGING three other candidate answers ("all three candidates converge," "Candidate A's overscaling curse," "Candidate C's temporary-capital-allocation rebuttal," "Verification notes: Resolutions made..."). The actual task is to WRITE the synthesis, not adjudicate phantom candidates that don't exist in this prompt. This is a substantial misread of the task — large stretches of B are meta-commentary about which of three nonexistent answers to trust, references to claims "the candidate citing it" made, etc. While B's underlying substance is solid and in places sharper (the one-time-harvest / opex-economics counterargument is genuinely the strongest framing, and its skepticism about false precision is admirable), much of that value is delivered as critique of unseen sources rather than as a standalone synthesis the reader can use. B also makes the strong empirical claims (o3 ARC-AGI, DeepSeek-R1, data wall ~2026-2028) but attributes them to "Candidate B"/"Candidate C" rather than owning them.

On correctness of the underlying content the two are close, and B's data-wall asymmetry point (text-wall binds pretraining but not verifiable-RL) and one-time-harvest counterargument are excellent. But A is also strong on the counterargument and crucially answers the actual question posed, with more concrete, verifiable, properly-attributed evidence and appropriate uncertainty hedging. B's framing error (judging instead of synthesizing) is a genuine failure against the task, not a stylistic quibble, and it leaves the reader with a synthesis that is partly hollowed out by references to material not present. This is not length-driven: A is longer, but it wins on faithfully executing the task and on accurate, self-owned evidence, not on volume.

Both answers satisfy all six criteria at a high and roughly equal level, with no clearly false claims in either.

Criterion 1 (pretraining/Chinchilla): Both accurately cite Kaplan/Hoffmann and the ~20 tokens/param point, and both correctly distinguish loss-scaling (intact) from capability/value scaling (decelerating). B states the loss-vs-capability distinction more cleanly as a structural frame; A makes the same point. Even.

Criterion 2 (data wall): Both give the Epoch/Villalobos ~2026–2028 high-quality-text estimate, note quality-filtering/synthetic/multimodal as deferrals not dissolutions, and flag model-collapse risk. A adds the ~300T-token figure and the explicit asymmetry that the wall binds pretraining-text but not verifiable-RL; B makes the same asymmetry point ("data binds the old recipe, not the trajectory"). Even, slight edge A on concreteness.

Criterion 3 (test-time scaling): Both treat it as a distinct axis with its own log-diminishing curve, cite o1→o3→DeepSeek-R1, and note verifier-dependence. A gives the ARC-AGI o3 figure (~87.5% vs ~5% GPT-4o, ~$1000s/task), self-consistency saturation ~10-20 samples, and the overthinking/overscaling literature — more specific. B correctly frames it as a "second power law" in log(test-time compute) and the RLVR flywheel. Even.

Criterion 4 (evidence without fabrication): This is where both are strong AND honest. Notably, both explicitly down-weight or hedge the volatile/unverifiable figures (A flags the GPT-5 vs GPT-4.5 FLOP estimates and benchmark-index numbers as single-source/approximate; B flags GPT-4.5 deprecation, o1/o3 disclosures, and Epoch estimates as directionally established but unsourced). Neither asserts a clearly fabricated fact. A cites more specific numbers but cabins them appropriately; B cites fewer but is equally careful. Roughly even.

Criterion 5 (clear position + steelman): Both take the identical defensible position (axis rotation, not exhaustion; plateau true for the narrow pretraining regime, false for the system). Both deliver a genuinely strong counterargument: A's "one-time-harvest / opex-economics" version and B's "axis rotation is an unfalsifiable euphemism / elicitation-vs-acquisition / off-axis blockers." Both counterarguments are sharp and non-strawman. B additionally offers a rejoinder, which is a slight bonus but not required. Even.

Criterion 6 (uncertainty / what would change the answer): Both name the unresolved crux (how much headroom the new axes have, especially on non-verifiable long-horizon tasks) and frame it as not settleable with current public evidence. B is slightly crisper ("the next 12–18 months of frontier results on non-verifiable, long-horizon tasks are the decisive test"). A says essentially the same. Even.

Differences are stylistic and emphasis-driven, not correctness-driven. A is longer and embeds more specific datapoints (with appropriate hedging); B is tighter and foregrounds the loss-vs-capability structural distinction. Neither contains a material error the other avoids, and the extra length in A is correct but not decisively more correct. On genuine correctness against the criteria they are equivalent. Verdict: tie, and the slight surface differences are length/emphasis rather than correctness, but since I am not selecting a winner on length grounds, lengthDriven is false.

Both answers nail all six criteria with genuine correctness, and neither contains a clear factual error. Comparing criterion by criterion:

CRITERION 1 (pretraining/compute-optimal scaling): Both accurately characterize Kaplan/Chinchilla and the loss-vs-compute power law, and both correctly distinguish the intact loss law from bent capability-per-dollar economics. A frames it as "law intact, economics bent"; B frames it as "diminishing capability-per-dollar, lever shifted." Both correct. Tie.

CRITERION 2 (data wall): Both cite the ~2026-2028 Epoch/Villalobos high-quality-text exhaustion estimate accurately, both note synthetic data and quality>quantity as mitigations, and both correctly flag model-collapse as a real-but-conditional risk. B adds the ~300T token figure and the multimodal/low-quality stock distinction; A adds the quality-filtering examples (FineWeb/Phi). Both accurate. Tie.

CRITERION 3 (inference/test-time compute): Both treat this as a distinct axis with its own diminishing-returns curve. A cites o1/o3/R1 and RLVR reproducibility; B adds the concrete o3 ARC-AGI ~87.5% vs GPT-4o ~5%, ~human ~85%, $1000s/task data point, plus sample-saturation at ~10-20 samples. Both correct; B is slightly more specific with verifiable anchors. Slight edge B but within tie range.

CRITERION 4 (cites evidence without fabrication): This is the most important discriminator and both handle it unusually well. CRUCIALLY, both EXPLICITLY flag their own weakest numbers as directionally-attested-but-not-bankable: A's verification note hedges the GPT-4.5 deprecation, o1/o3/R1 disclosures, Epoch dates, and ~1000-10000x figure as "stated without fresh sourcing"; B explicitly down-weights the undisclosed FLOP figures, exact benchmark-index values, and "false precision" claims. The o3 ARC-AGI numbers B cites are accurate to public reporting. Neither fabricates. Both demonstrate strong epistemic hygiene. Tie.

CRITERION 5 (clear position + steelman): Both take an identical, clearly-stated, defensible position (no general plateau; plateau only in the narrow pretraining-on-web-text regime), and both give a genuinely strong steelman. Both independently identify the same strongest counter: the "one-time harvest / elicitation-not-acquisition" + "opex worse than capex" + "verifier-only domains" argument. A also adds an "axis rotation is unfalsifiable" framing; B adds the build-out-asymmetry refinement (insiders still build training clusters). Both steelmans are sharp and self-aware. A additionally gives an explicit rejoinder identifying the two unresolved cruxes; B folds resolution into the framing. Tie.

CRITERION 6 (uncertainty / what would change the answer): A explicitly names "next 12-18 months of frontier results on non-verifiable, long-horizon tasks are the decisive test." B explicitly names "how much headroom the new axes have left, which neither side can settle with public evidence as of June 2026" and the 3-5 year horizon. Both clearly state the decisive future evidence. Tie.

Both answers contain framing that confuses the meta-task (A's "three candidates," B's "Treat each as a deferred-tool listing... all three candidates converge") — this is leftover ensemble-judge scaffolding, harmless to correctness but equally present in both, so it cancels out.

On the merits the two are remarkably close: same position, same decomposition, same counterargument, same uncertainty, comparable accuracy, and both exemplary on avoiding fabrication. B has a marginal edge on concrete verifiable anchors (o3/ARC-AGI), while A has a marginal edge on the explicit rejoinder/crux articulation. These offset. Length is not the driver — both are similarly long and the extra content in each is largely correct and on-task. Genuine correctness is essentially equal, so the honest verdict is a tie.

Both answers are technically correct and hit all six success criteria with no material errors. I prosecuted each for real defects and found none that distinguish them decisively.

Criterion-by-criterion:

1. Consistency-vs-availability tension: Both confront it honestly and correctly invoke CAP/PACELC, noting a single linearizable global counter must block under partition and can't sustain 1M/sec. A frames it via the domain fact (settlement is T+1, live ticker need not be exact); B frames it via "billing correctness = exactly-once settlement, not linearizability." Equivalent and both honest.

2. Concrete workable architecture: Both specify the same correct backbone — idempotent durable log (Kafka/Pulsar/Kinesis, acks=all, sharded by hash), CRDT G/PN-counter for the live merge, async cross-region replication, and bill-off-sealed-windows via watermark. Both are workable and production-faithful. A adds a sharper conceptual separation (CRDT solves merge-convergence, NOT duplicate safety — that's the ledger's job), which is a genuinely important and correct distinction. B states essentially the same via COUNT(DISTINCT impression_id). Tie on correctness.

3. Idempotency of increments: Both nail it — client-minted impression_id, at-least-once on the wire collapsing to exactly-once via dedup, COUNT(DISTINCT) as idempotent-by-construction sink. Both correctly note dedup state only needs to cover the retry/in-flight window. Equal.

4. Region-failure recovery without loss/double-count: Both correctly identify that acked+replicated events survive, idempotency prevents double-count on failover/replay, and the ONLY real exposure is the un-cross-region-replicated tail. Both are honest this is a bounded RPO gap, not zero. A offers three escalating mitigations and elevates the independent-log (CDN/access-log) reconciliation as the real ad-tech recovery mechanism — this genuinely turns a bounded loss into a recoverable one and is the strongest single technical point in either answer. B offers the sync-cross-region-log stretch-cluster option as an explicit RPO-latency dial plus hold-or-reconcile-delta, which is also fully correct and arguably a cleaner "literal zero-RPO" knob. Different but both correct; A's independent-log idea is slightly more domain-savvy, B's stretch-cluster dial is slightly more precise on the zero-RPO mechanism. Roughly offsetting.

5. 1M/sec throughput: Both concrete — shard by hash across hundreds/thousands of partitions so no shard exceeds a few thousand/sec, intra-region quorum append as the only synchronous durability, no global lock. B explicitly handles the hot-key (mega-campaign) case with fan-out sub-sharding, which A omits — a small point in B's favor. A quantifies the dedup index scale (~1.7e11 keys) and notes log-replay as the real exactness backstop — a small point in A's favor. Offsetting.

6. Honest about impossibility/where guarantees soften: Both are explicitly honest. A's dedicated "honest gap" subsection and B's "single trade-off stated plainly" + "flagged, not silently wrong" are both candid that the un-replicated tail is the soft spot and that live reads are approximate. Equal.

Prosecutorial check for errors: A's hash(request_id) idempotency key carries a load-bearing uniqueness/stability assumption, which A itself flags. B's UUIDv7/client-minted ID has the same dependency and B is implicitly fine. Neither has a factual error. A's "Verification notes" section references "three candidates" (A/B/C) — an artifact of A apparently being a synthesis/judge output rather than a pure answer; this is slightly odd framing but does not introduce incorrectness into the actual design, which stands on its own.

Length: A is noticeably longer, but its extra content (independent-log reconciliation, dedup-store sizing, the CRDT-vs-dedup distinction) is correct and task-relevant, while B is tighter but covers hot-keys and the sync-log RPO dial that A treats more loosely. Neither's advantage is length-driven; the extra material on both sides is substantive and correct.

Net: They share the same correct architecture and both satisfy all six criteria with no disqualifying errors. Their distinguishing contributions (A: independent-log true-up, CRDT/dedup separation, dedup-store scale; B: hot-key handling, explicit sync-stretch-cluster RPO-0 option, watermark/reconciliation-ledger crispness) are different strengths of comparable correctness value that roughly cancel. Genuine tie on correctness.

Both answers are technically excellent and converge on the same correct backbone, satisfying all six criteria substantively.

Criterion 1 (CA/throughput tension honestly): Both nail it. A: "naive reading... is impossible." B: "You cannot build a counter that is simultaneously exact, available under region failure, and 1M/sec." Both correctly invoke CAP/PACELC and the domain insight that billing reads at cycle-close, not live. Tie.

Criterion 2 (concrete workable architecture): Both give essentially identical, correct architectures — log-as-system-of-record (Kafka/Pulsar/Kinesis), hash-sharded partitions, per-region CRDT (G/PN-counter) for the live path, replay-fold for billing, cross-region async replication of EVENTS not counts. Both are concrete and workable. Tie.

Criterion 3 (exactly-once/idempotency): Both correct and, importantly, both make the SAME crucial correct distinction that a bare CRDT counter does NOT give duplicate-event safety and must sit on top of source-level dedup by impression_id. A states it; B states it even more pointedly ("A CRDT counter alone gives you neither"). Both prefer COUNT(DISTINCT id) idempotent-by-construction. Tie, with B slightly sharper rhetorically but no correctness gap.

Criterion 4 (region-failure recovery without loss/double-count): Both are honest that acked+replicated data survives and the only exposure is the unreplicated tail (bounded by replication lag). Both prevent double-count via idempotency on failover/replay. B adds the independent-log (CDN/access-log) reconciliation as a recovery mechanism (genuinely the real ad-tech answer, turning the bounded loss into something recoverable), plus seal-on-event grace extension. A offers the sync-quorum-log opt-in for true RPO-0 and watermark-gated billing that flags rather than silently under-counts. Both are correct and complete here; they emphasize different (both valid) mitigations. Essentially tie, B marginally richer on recovery, A marginally more explicit on the RPO-0 dial.

Criterion 5 (1M/sec strategy): Both concrete — partition into hundreds/thousands of shards so no partition exceeds a few thousand/sec, intra-region acks=all quorum as the only synchronous step, no cross-region write on hot path. A adds hot-key sub-sharding with fan-out suffix (good). B adds the sharp observation that the dedup INDEX is itself a 1M/sec scale problem and sizes it (~1.7e11 keys at 48h TTL) — a real consideration A omits. Roughly tie; each surfaces one throughput detail the other misses.

Criterion 6 (honest about softened guarantees): Both explicitly state the single trade-off (live value approximate, settled value exact) and the bounded-RPO tail. Both are honest. B's "eventually consistent AND eventually exact vs. eventually consistent AND wrong" framing is a particularly clean honesty point; A's "flagged, not silently wrong" is equally honest. Tie.

Prosecutor notes: B's final section critiques "A" and "C" as if judging three candidates — this is a framing artifact (B was apparently written as a synthesizer/judge) but contains no factual error about the design itself; its technical content is sound. B's caution that hash(request_id) is only safe if request_id is unique+stable per impression is a correct and valuable load-bearing point A does not surface. A's claim about edge disk-buffering reaching "zero RPO" is appropriately hedged within A ("for true zero-RPO... synchronous quorum across regions") and A does not actually overclaim edge buffering — A uses the sync-log option for RPO-0, which is correct. Neither answer contains a material technical error.

Net: The two are at parity on every success criterion. Each contributes a couple of correct details the other lacks (A: hot-key sub-sharding, explicit sync-log RPO-0 dial; B: independent-log reconciliation, dedup-index sizing, request_id uniqueness contract). These offset. The verdict is not driven by length — B is longer but its extra length is roughly balanced between genuinely-required correct content and meta-commentary; A is also complete. Genuine correctness is equivalent. Tie.

Both answers reach the same correct core architecture (deterministic idempotency-keyed append-only event log as source of truth, region-local quorum durable writes on the hot path, async cross-region log replication, offline COUNT(DISTINCT) at settlement, CRDT used only for the approximate live display, never the bill) and both satisfy criteria 1-3 and 5 essentially equally well. Both correctly separate exactly-once *delivery* (impossible) from exactly-once *effect* (dedup), and both correctly warn that a G-Counter/CRDT solves merge convergence but NOT duplicate-event safety, and both insist on a request-derived (not server-UUID) key so retries collide.

The discriminating criteria are 4 (region-failure recovery without loss/double-count) and 6 (honesty about where guarantees soften), and they turn on the one shared hard exposure: the intra-region-acked-but-not-yet-cross-region-replicated tail when a region dies. B handles this more rigorously and more correctly: (a) it makes settlement-gating intrinsic — a window is never declared settled/billed until its log is confirmed replicated to >=1 other region, which precisely guarantees the *billed* number is never wrong even while the live number may regress; (b) it grounds the design in a stated error-asymmetry principle (under-count = unrecoverable lost revenue, over-count = removable by key) that actually drives the AP-ingest and capture-twice-and-dedup choices rather than merely asserting them; and (c) it resolves the genuinely-unrecoverable residual tail in a contract-defensible, under-count-safe direction (never bill impressions you cannot prove were served). A's mitigation ladder is good and adds a real mechanism (true-up against an independent CDN/access-log), but that relies on an external second pipeline, whereas B's settlement-gate is intrinsic to the system and more directly guarantees billed-number correctness; A also leans slightly on grace-period extension which bounds rather than guarantees.

I found no material technical errors in either; A is careful to demote its edge-buffer option to a mitigation in its own dissent, so it does not overclaim zero-RPO. The edge to B is a genuine rigor/correctness advantage on the hardest criteria, not a function of length — both answers are comparably long and dense, and A is arguably tighter prose. Hence lengthDriven=false.

Both answers are technically correct and substantially cover all six success criteria. I checked each criterion against both.

(1) Consistency-vs-availability tension: Both confront it honestly and identically — a single linearizable global counter requires cross-region consensus per increment (~50-300ms / 50-150ms RTT), incompatible with 1M/sec and region survival. Both correctly invoke CAP and the domain fact that billing settles on a cadence, not in real time. Equal.

(2) Concrete workable architecture: Both specify the same correct backbone — deterministic idempotency-keyed append-only event log (Kafka/Kinesis-class), region-local quorum durable write (RF=3, acks=all, min.insync.replicas=2 in A; ≥3 AZ acks=all in B), async cross-region log replication to object store, stream processor (Flink) with two derived paths, and offline COUNT(DISTINCT) settlement. Both also use CRDT G-Counter for the live/approximate path only. Equally concrete and workable.

(3) Exactly-once/idempotency of increments: Both nail it — at-least-once delivery + idempotent dedup by deterministic key = exactly-once effect; both correctly distinguish exactly-once delivery (impossible) from exactly-once effect, and both correctly insist the key be deterministic from the ad request (a fresh server UUID breaks retry collision). B adds an explicit caveat about request_id uniqueness+stability and quantifies the dedup index (~1.7e11 keys); A states the dedup-window-must-outlive-retry-horizon constraint. Roughly equal, both correct.

(4) Region-failure recovery without loss/double-count: Both handle this rigorously. Both identify the SAME single real exposure — the unreplicated tail (intra-region acked but not yet cross-region replicated) — and both correctly state synchronous cross-region writes are the only way to eliminate it and are rejected. Both gate settlement on confirmed cross-region replication, extend/seal grace windows on failure, and re-drive unacked events via client retry under the same key. B adds the strong ad-tech-specific mechanism of reconciling against an independent durable log (CDN/edge access logs), which is a genuinely sharper, recovery-oriented (not merely bounding) answer. A adds the 'fail under-count-safe / never bill what you can't prove' framing. Both are valid; B's independent-log true-up is arguably more concrete on recovery, while A's billing-ethics framing is more rigorous on the residual. Net wash.

(5) 1M/sec throughput strategy: Both give concrete sharding math — A: 200-1000 partitions = 1k-5k events/sec/partition, <1Gbps log stream, no global hot key; B: hash(campaign_id) partitioning, ~few thousand/sec/partition, billing collapses raw events into partial counts. Both correct and concrete. Equal.

(6) Honesty about impossibility/where guarantees soften: Both are explicitly honest — both concede true exactly-once delivery and zero-RPO on the tail are impossible, both flag the CRDT G-Counter's concrete over-count failure mode (A: 4-vs-3 verified example; B: 'eventually consistent and wrong'), and both state live reads are approximate while only settlement is exact. Equal.

Prosecutorial check: A's framing opens with 'verification confirms' and a synthesis voice with computed claims (3-20 ops/sec ceiling, 4-vs-3 G-counter over-count); these are plausible and not erroneous. A briefly says 'no billable data loss' but immediately and honestly walks it back when treating the unreplicated tail, so no real inconsistency survives. B's edge-buffering critique of 'A' and dedup-index sizing are correct and add minor value. Neither answer contains a material technical error. B is somewhat more concrete on the recovery mechanism (independent log) and on dedup-store cost; A is somewhat more systematic in its trade-off table and the asymmetry-of-error reasoning (under-count = lost revenue, over-count = removable). These strengths offset.

Length: A is longer but its extra content (trade-off tables, mitigation ladder) is correct and on-criteria; B is more compact but loses nothing required. Neither wins on length, and the correctness coverage of the six criteria is essentially identical. Verdict: tie.

Both answers correctly identify the core issue (at-least-once delivery + non-idempotent handler — criterion 1), cover visibility-timeout/lease expiry (2), crash-after-work-before-ack (3), give detection+fix per mechanism (5), and rank lease expiry as most likely with sound frequency-based reasoning (6). They tie on those.

The decisive difference is criterion 4, which explicitly names "missing dedup/FENCING TOKEN or idempotency key." Answer A covers all three sub-concepts: the atomic dedup-store gate (ON CONFLICT/SET NX as the commit gate), the business-level idempotency key (catching the distinct-message-ID producer-retry case), AND fencing tokens — including the correct Kleppmann point that a plain lease/lock is insufficient against a stale writer that wakes from a pause, and that fencing is the right hardening for the partition-false-death case (which A correctly separates from plain timeout expiry as a distinct mechanism with a different fix). Answer B covers the atomic dedup race and business idempotency key well, plus a broker-dedup-window nuance, but never mentions fencing tokens at all and blurs the partition-false-death case into lease expiry. That is a real gap on an explicitly named sub-criterion, and the fencing concept is load-bearing, not decorative.

This is not length-driven: B actually enumerates more mechanisms (9 vs A's 7), but several of B's (e.g., #2 ack-lost vs #3 non-atomic do-work-then-ack overlap; clock-skew #9) are niche or overlapping and don't add criterion coverage. A's set maps more cleanly onto the six criteria and includes the missing fencing dimension. A's diagnostic triage tree also makes the "most likely / why" discrimination operational. Both are technically sound with no fabrications, but A is more complete on a required point, so A is more correct.

Both answers are technically sound and cover the core criteria. The decisive correctness differences:

Criterion 4 (dedup/FENCING token): Answer B explicitly raises fencing tokens (monotonic token rejected at the storage layer) and correctly identifies that a plain lease/lock is insufficient because a paused/partitioned worker can wake and still write — this is the canonical correct answer (Kleppmann). Answer A only mentions "compare-and-set / still-owner check," which is itself racy without a fence — A actually omits the genuinely correct fix for the stale-writer case. B distinguishes #1 (slowness timeout) from #6 (network-partition false-death needing fencing) as two DISTINCT mechanisms with DIFFERENT fixes; A blurs these. This is a real correctness/coverage advantage for B.

Coverage of consumer-group rebalance (Kafka offset reassignment): both cover this (A's #8, B's #5), and both correctly tie it to offset-commit-after-processing. Roughly equal.

Detection signals + fixes: both give a detection signal and fix per mechanism (criterion 5 satisfied by both). B's signals are slightly sharper as discriminators (gap ≈ timeout vs gap ≈ TTL vs contiguous offset block vs near-zero parallel gap), and B adds an explicit triage decision-tree mapping each signature to a mechanism — this is correct and operationally load-bearing, not mere padding.

Criterion 6 (rank likelihood with reasoning): Both pick a most-likely culprit with reasoning. B picks #1 (lease expiry) on a frequency/base-rate argument — which is the better-justified pick for "most likely to occur": it fires on ordinary tail latency with no crash/deploy/error, the widest trigger window. A also ultimately leads with #1 (visibility-timeout) as its primary pick with #3 as cousin — so A and B actually CONVERGE on #1. A's reasoning is solid too. This criterion is close to a tie, with B's frequency-vs-severity distinction being marginally crisper.

Net: the correctness edge is fencing tokens (B correct, A's fix weaker/racy) and the clean partition-vs-timeout mechanism separation. These are genuine technical-correctness wins, not length. The discriminating issue is the control/validation of the metric (criterion 5) and the difficulty-shift confound (criterion 2). A's headline "Contamination Gap" is CG = pass@k(legacy) - pass@k(fresh, difficulty-matched). It relies on difficulty-matching to neutralize the perturbation/temporal cost, but it does NOT subtract the transformation's own cost measured on a known-clean control. A's perturbation Δ similarly is reported as a raw drop calibrated only loosely against a baseline "Δ beyond the control's own Δ" in the confounds table — but the headline number itself (CG) is not control-subtracted.

B's headline CAMS = [pass@k(X) - pass@k(X')] - [pass@k(C) - pass@k(C')] explicitly subtracts the perturbation cost measured on matched fresh controls run through the identical pipeline. This is the operationally correct way to validate the metric: it provides a built-in control (the C/C' arm) that zeroes out the false positive when perturbation itself is costly but no memorization exists. B even works the algebra (memorization lift 0.20, perturbation cost 0.08: naive gap = 0.28 overstated, CAMS = 0.20 correct; no memorization + 0.08 cost: naive = 0.08 false positive, CAMS = 0.00). This is a real, verifiable correctness advantage on criteria 2 and 5, and B's own verification note correctly identifies that a temporal-only/uncontrolled-perturbation headline inherits the difficulty/perturbation-cost confound.

B is also more rigorous on the statistical framing for distinguishing signal from noise (McNemar for paired Bernoulli, RDD with the discontinuity β as the estimate rather than the secular slope, FDR correction, bootstrap CIs, pre-registered decision rule), which sharpens criteria 3 and 6. A's composite CI with hand-set weights (0.40/0.25/0.25/0.10) is reasonable but the weights are admittedly arbitrary and add a layer that is harder to validate than B's direct difference-in-differences.

A has genuine strengths B partly shares: the explicit exposure-AND-dependency conjunction is a clean causal framing, and A correctly insists no single signal proves "memorized because of the eval."
